title: Getting Started with JASP
text: JASP is free, open-source statistical software developed at the University of Amsterdam. It is designed to make statistical analyses intuitive and interactive — output updates in real time as you change settings.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Download and install JASP
Go to jasp-stats.org/download and download the installer for your operating system (Windows, macOS, or Linux). Installation is straightforward; no licence is required.






TipWhich version?



Always download the latest stable release. If you are working on a university computer that already has JASP installed, make sure it is version 0.18 or later — some modules used in this course require a recent version.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Loading data into JASP
JASP can open several file formats: .jasp (JASP’s own format), .csv, .sav (SPSS), .xlsx, .ods, and more.
Opening a .jasp file — this is the easiest way to practise. A .jasp file contains both the dataset and any pre-configured analyses. Double-click the file or use File → Open in JASP.
Opening a .csv or .sav file — use File → Open and navigate to your file. After opening, JASP shows the spreadsheet. Check that your variables have the correct measurement level (nominal, ordinal, or scale) by clicking the variable type icon next to the column header.

Getting started video
▶ Removing an outlier in JASP — a short example from the JASP Video Library.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
The JASP interface
When you open a dataset, the JASP window is divided into three panels:







Panel
What it shows




Left — Data
The spreadsheet with your variables and cases


Middle — Analysis menu
The list of available statistical tests


Right — Output
Results that update in real time as you configure the analysis




Variable types
JASP uses three measurement levels:

Nominal — unordered categories (e.g., treatment group, gender)
Ordinal — ordered categories (e.g., Likert scales treated as ordinal)
Scale (continuous/interval/ratio) — numeric measurements (e.g., reaction time, score)

Assigning the correct variable type matters because JASP uses it to determine which analyses are available and how to display your data.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Running an analysis

Select the relevant menu item (e.g., T-Tests → Independent Samples T-Test)
Drag variables into the appropriate boxes (dependent variable, grouping variable)
Tick the options you need (effect size, assumption checks, plots)
Output appears immediately in the right panel

There is no “Run” button — JASP updates results live.

title: Getting Started with JASP
text:
Saving your work

File → Save saves a .jasp file containing both data and current analyses
To export the output, click the downward triangle (▼) next to any result table or figure and choose Copy, Save as image, or similar

Check the variable types in the column headers, add value labels for the singer factor, and produce a basic descriptive summary.

title: Power Analysis
text:
Advanced Topics
A power analysis determines the sample size needed to detect an effect of a given size with a specified probability, given a chosen significance threshold. Running a power analysis before data collection is a core component of preregistration and transparent science.

title: Power Analysis
text:
Key concepts
Statistical power (1 − β) is the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis. Conventional minimum: .80 (80%), meaning a 20% chance of a false negative.
Four quantities are mathematically related — if you know three, you can calculate the fourth:



Quantity
Symbol
Typical default




Sample size
N
To be determined


Effect size
d, f, r, etc.
From prior research or theory


Significance level
α
.05


Power
1 − β
.80



Most commonly: specify the expected effect size, α = .05, power = .80 → solve for N.

title: Power Analysis
text:
Effect sizes for power analysis
Use the effect size metric appropriate for your planned analysis:



Analysis
Effect size




t-test
Cohen’s d


ANOVA
Cohen’s f (or η²: f = √(η²/(1−η²)))


Correlation / regression
Pearson’s r or Cohen’s f²


Chi-square
Cohen’s w



Where do I get the expected effect size?

Previous studies: the effect size from published research on the same or closely related topic
Meta-analyses: if one exists for your research question, use the meta-analytic estimate
Pilot data: run a small pilot study first (though pilot estimates are unreliable with small N)
Smallest effect size of interest (SESOI): what is the minimum effect that would be theoretically or practically meaningful? This is increasingly recommended over using published estimates, which may be inflated.







WarningPublication bias inflates effect sizes



Published effect sizes are often inflated due to selective reporting. Using them naively will lead to underpowered studies. Where possible, use meta-analytic estimates or pre-specify a conservative (smaller) effect size.

title: Power Analysis
text:
Tools for power analysis

G*Power (recommended)
G*Power is free, widely used, and covers all common analyses including t-tests, ANOVA, regression, and correlation.
Steps: 1. Select Test family (e.g., t-tests) 2. Select Statistical test (e.g., Means: Difference between two independent means) 3. Set Type of power analysis to A priori (for planning) 4. Enter effect size, α, and desired power → click Calculate


JASP: jpower module
JASP includes a jpower module for power analysis. Install it via Modules (☰) → Available Modules → jpower.


Online tools

pwr2ppl — Shiny app for a variety of designs
Superpower — power for ANOVA designs including repeated measures
WebPower — comprehensive online power calculator

title: Power Analysis
text:
Sensitivity analysis
If your sample size is already fixed (e.g., a clinical trial limited by patient availability), conduct a sensitivity analysis: given my N, α, and desired power, what is the minimum effect size I can detect? This helps set realistic expectations.

title: Power Analysis
text:
Reporting power analysis






TipAPA reporting example



A priori power analysis (GPower 3.1) indicated that a sample of N* = 128 (64 per group) would provide 80% power to detect a medium-sized effect (d = 0.50) with α = .05 (two-tailed independent-samples t-test).

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Advanced Topics
Mediation and moderation both involve a third variable in the relationship between a predictor (X) and an outcome (Y) — but they ask fundamentally different questions.
In JASP, both are estimated using the PROCESS module, developed by Andrew Hayes.

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Mediation

What is it?
Mediation tests whether the effect of X on Y is transmitted through a third variable M (the mediator).
X → M → Y      (indirect effect)
X → Y          (direct effect, controlling for M)
The total effect of X on Y is decomposed into:

Direct effect: the effect of X on Y after accounting for M
Indirect effect (a × b): the effect of X on Y that goes through M (= path a × path b)







TipDecomposing the total effect





The trick of mediation: the original X → Y relationship gets split into two pathways. The direct effect (X → Y, controlling for M) and the indirect effect (X → M → Y). A relationship that’s completely mediated by M tells a very different psychological story than one with no mediation at all.





Assumptions


The causal ordering X → M → Y must be theoretically justified — mediation analysis does not prove causality
All variables are measured without error (impossible in practice, but a key limitation to acknowledge)
There is no unmeasured confounding of the X → M, M → Y, or X → Y relationships
No interaction between X and M — if you suspect one, use moderated mediation (Hayes Model 8 or 14)




Inference: bootstrapping
The indirect effect (a × b) is non-normally distributed. JASP uses bootstrapped confidence intervals (typically 5000–10000 samples). An indirect effect is considered significant if the 95% CI does not contain zero.


How to do it in JASP
Install the PROCESS module: Modules (☰) → Available Modules → PROCESS.






NoteJASP Video Library — Mediation



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_PROCESS.html#mediation:

▶ Load Module — installing and opening the PROCESS module
▶ Specify Model — assigning X, M, and Y; bootstrapped indirect effects
▶ Hayes Model — using pre-defined Hayes templates (Model 4 for simple mediation)





Interpreting mediation output







Path
What to look for




Path a
X → M: is the predictor associated with the mediator?


Path b
M → Y: does the mediator predict the outcome, controlling for X?


Direct effect (c’)
X → Y controlling for M: has the effect changed from the total effect?


Indirect effect (a × b)
Is the 95% bootstrapped CI excluding zero?



Full mediation: the direct effect becomes non-significant once M is included — X only affects Y through M. Partial mediation: both direct and indirect effects remain significant.






TipAPA reporting



Report the indirect effect with its bootstrapped CI: indirect effect = [value], 95% CI [lower, upper]. For the direct and total effects, report b, SE, t, p.

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Moderation

What is it?
Moderation tests whether the effect of X on Y differs across levels of a third variable W (the moderator). Statistically, this is an interaction effect: you add the product term X × W to the regression model.
If the X × W interaction is significant, the slope of X on Y is different at different values of W.






TipModeration = interaction = “steering”





A useful mnemonic: to moderate means to steer — to control how something unfolds. Statistically, moderation is just an interaction effect: one predictor steers how another predictor affects the outcome. Which of the two predictors gets called “the moderator” is somewhat arbitrary — it’s about which framing matches your theoretical question.





Assumptions


The same as multiple regression — see the Regression page
Centering: for continuous moderators, it is good practice to mean-centre X and W before computing the product term. This reduces multicollinearity and makes the lower-order coefficients interpretable. JASP does this automatically




Simple slopes and interaction plots
After a significant moderation, inspect simple slopes: the slope of X on Y at specific values of W (typically M − 1 SD, M, M + 1 SD for a continuous moderator). JASP provides simple slope plots and significance tests.


How to do it in JASP






NoteJASP Video Library — Moderation



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_PROCESS.html#moderation:

▶ Specify Model — entering X, W, and the X×W product term
▶ Hayes Model 1 — simple moderation using a Hayes template
▶ Flexplot — visualising the interaction
▶ Simple Slopes Plot — effect of X at low/medium/high levels of W

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Moderated mediation
When moderation and mediation are combined — the indirect effect of X on Y through M depends on the level of W — this is moderated mediation (or conditional indirect effect). Hayes’ Model 8 and Model 14 are the most common configurations.
The indirect effect is estimated separately at different values of W. A Johnson-Neyman interval identifies the exact range of W values for which the indirect effect is significant.

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Further reading

DSUJ Chapter 10 — Moderation and mediation — comprehensive treatment with PROCESS walkthroughs
Hayes, A. F. (2022). Introduction to mediation, moderation, and conditional process analysis — the definitive text

title: Mediation & Moderation
text:
Try it yourself
Each exercise below ends with a collapsible solution. Try the analysis first, then expand to compare.

Exercise 1 (mediation) — Does mate value mediate the age → gossiping relationship?
Older adults gossip more than younger adults — but is this transmitted through changes in perceived mate value? Using leni_10.jasp, fit a Hayes Model 4 mediation with age as X, mate value as M, and gossiping tendency as Y. Report the indirect effect with its bootstrapped 95% CI.






Note▸ Worked solution






Walkthrough: Labcoat Leni — I heard that Jane has a boil and kissed a tramp
Annotated JASP output: leni_10.html






Exercise 2 (moderation) — Does the attractiveness → spousal-support relationship differ for husbands vs wives?
McNulty et al. (2008) tested whether attractiveness predicts how much support newlyweds give their partner — and whether this relationship is moderated by being the husband vs the wife. Using mcnulty_2008.jasp, fit a Hayes Model 1 moderation with attractiveness as the continuous predictor, spouse as the categorical moderator, and support as the outcome. Inspect the simple slopes.






Note▸ Worked solution






Walkthrough: Smart Alex Task 10.1
Annotated JASP output: alex_10_01-03.html






Exercise 3 (mediation) — Does product-perceived-coolness mediate the advert → desirability path?
A company owner measured how cool people thought the advertising was, how cool they thought the product was, and how desirable they found the product. Using tablets.jasp, test whether the relationship between advertising coolness and product desirability is mediated by product coolness.






Note▸ Worked solution






Walkthrough: Smart Alex Task 10.5
Annotated JASP output: alex_10_05.html

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Advanced Topics
Meta-analysis is a statistical method for synthesising effect sizes across multiple studies. Rather than relying on a single study, a meta-analysis pools estimates to arrive at a more precise, generalisable conclusion about an effect.

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
When to use meta-analysis

You have collected effect sizes (or sufficient statistics) from multiple independent studies on the same research question
Your thesis includes a systematic review with a quantitative synthesis component
You want to quantify heterogeneity — do effect sizes vary across studies, and if so, why?

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Key concepts

Effect size metrics
Choose the effect size metric appropriate for your data:



Data type
Effect size metric




Two group means (continuous DV)
Cohen’s d / Hedges’ g


Correlational data
Fisher’s z-transformed r


Frequencies / proportions
Log odds ratio (log OR)


Means across time / conditions
Standardised mean change





Fixed-effects vs. random-effects models








Model
Assumption
Use when




Fixed-effects
All studies estimate the same true effect
Exact replications — rarely appropriate


Random-effects
Studies estimate different true effects drawn from a distribution
Different studies, different populations — almost always preferred



In a random-effects model, the variance of the distribution of true effects (τ²) is estimated. A larger τ² indicates more heterogeneity.


Heterogeneity







Statistic
What it tells you




Q
Total variability in effect sizes; significant Q indicates heterogeneity beyond sampling error


I²
Percentage of total variability due to true heterogeneity (not sampling error). I² = 25% (low), 50% (moderate), 75% (high) as rough guides


τ (tau)
The standard deviation of the distribution of true effects — in the original effect size metric

title: Meta-Analysis
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP
Install the Meta-Analysis module: Modules (☰) → Available Modules → Meta-Analysis.
Then go to Meta-Analysis → Classical Meta-Analysis.






NoteJASP Video Library — Meta-Analysis



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Meta-Analysis.html:

▶ Effect Size Computation — computing d, r, or log OR from raw statistics
▶ Main Analysis — random-effects model, forest plot, heterogeneity statistics

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Reading a forest plot
A forest plot shows:

Each study as a horizontal line: the point estimate (square) and its confidence interval (the line)
The size of the square is proportional to the study’s weight (usually inverse variance)
The diamond at the bottom shows the pooled effect and its CI
The dashed vertical line is the pooled effect; the solid vertical line at 0 (or 1 for ORs) is the null

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Publication bias
Studies with null results are less likely to be published, which can inflate meta-analytic estimates. Assess with:

Funnel plot: plot effect size vs. standard error — asymmetry suggests publication bias
Egger’s test: a regression test for funnel plot asymmetry
Trim-and-fill method: imputes “missing” studies to correct for asymmetry

All of these are available in JASP’s meta-analysis module.

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Moderator analysis
If I² is high (> 50%), explore moderators — study-level variables (e.g., participant age, intervention type, publication year) that might explain heterogeneity. In JASP, add these as covariates in a meta-regression.

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Interpreting the output






TipAPA reporting example



A random-effects meta-analysis of k = 18 studies (total N = 2,341) yielded a pooled effect of d = 0.43, 95% CI [0.28, 0.58], z = 5.60, p < .001. Heterogeneity was moderate, Q(17) = 34.2, p = .007, I² = 50%. There was no evidence of publication bias based on Egger’s test (t(16) = 1.21, p = .24).

title: Meta-Analysis
text:
Further reading

Borenstein, M., Hedges, L. V., Higgins, J. P. T., & Rothstein, H. R. (2009). Introduction to meta-analysis. Wiley. — The standard text
Cochrane Handbook — comprehensive guide for systematic reviews in health research

text:

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
text:
What this page will cover
Latent Class Analysis (LCA) and Latent Profile Analysis (LPA) are person-centred approaches that identify subgroups (classes or profiles) of individuals who are similar to each other and different from other subgroups.








Technique
Variable type
Example




LCA
Categorical indicators
Classifying patients by symptom patterns (present/absent)


LPA
Continuous indicators
Identifying motivational profiles from several continuous scale scores



Both are forms of mixture modelling — the assumption is that the population is a mixture of k unobserved subgroups, and the goal is to identify those subgroups and assign individuals to them.
Key decisions: - How many classes? Use BIC, AIC, entropy, and the Lo-Mendell-Rubin test to decide - Are classes meaningful and interpretable? Statistical fit is necessary but not sufficient

title: Latent Class / Profile Analysis
text:
Useful resources in the meantime

Masyn, K. E. (2013). Latent class analysis and finite mixture modeling. In The Oxford handbook of quantitative methods (Vol. 2). Oxford University Press.
The Mplus software is frequently used for complex mixture models: statmodel.com

text: Advanced Topics

title: Path Modeling
text:
🚧
This page is under construction.
Content covering path modeling — and its relationship to SEM — will be added here.

title: Path Modeling
text:
What this page will cover
Path modeling extends multiple regression to systems of equations with multiple dependent variables. It allows you to:

Estimate direct and indirect effects simultaneously across a network of variables
Test theoretical models about causal pathways
Include latent variables (measured variables with error) — the combination of path modeling and latent variables is structural equation modeling (SEM)

Common applications in psychology: cross-lagged panel models (measuring variables at two time points to examine reciprocal relationships), growth curve models, and complex mediation models with multiple mediators.
Relationship to mediation: a simple mediation model (X → M → Y) is a path model. The Mediation & Moderation page covers simple cases using PROCESS. This page will cover more complex path structures.

title: Path Modeling
text:
Useful resources in the meantime

Kline, R. B. (2023). Principles and practice of structural equation modeling (5th ed.). Guilford Press.
The lavaan R package is widely used for SEM: lavaan.org

Start with the Mediation & Moderation and Factor Analysis pages before tackling full path models.

title: ANOVA
text:
Basics
Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) extends the logic of the t-test to situations with three or more groups, multiple factors, covariates, or repeated measurements. It answers the question: are there significant differences in means across conditions?
This page covers four ANOVA designs. Jump to the relevant section:

title: ANOVA
text:
One-way ANOVA

What is it?
One-way ANOVA tests whether the means of three or more independent groups differ on a continuous dependent variable. The “one-way” refers to a single grouping factor (independent variable).
The F-statistic is the ratio of between-groups variance to within-groups variance. A large F indicates that the group means are more spread out than expected by chance.






TipWhere the F-statistic comes from





F is the ratio of the mean squares of the model (how much variance the group means capture) to the mean squares of the error (leftover within-group variance). Like a t-statistic, F is a standardised test statistic with a known sampling distribution — that’s how a p-value gets attached to it.





Assumptions


Independence: each participant contributes data to only one group
Normality: the DV should be approximately normally distributed within each group (check with histograms or Q-Q plots per group)
Homogeneity of variance (homoscedasticity): the variance should be similar across groups — Levene’s test checks this. If violated, use Welch’s ANOVA (available in JASP)








TipWhy assumptions actually matter (small simulation)





When the DV is heavy-tailed (e.g. reaction times with extreme values), the empirical sampling distribution of F under H₀ no longer matches the theoretical F-distribution. The result: your α and power aren’t what you think they are. ANOVA is relatively robust to mild non-normality, but vulnerable to extreme observations that inflate within-group variance.
→ Simulation: what 100,000 ANOVAs under H₀ actually look like





Effect size
Report η² (eta squared) or the more conservative ω² (omega squared). Partial η² is often reported in factorial designs.



η²
Conventional label




.01
Small


.06
Medium


.14
Large









TipEffect size vs. test statistic — what’s the difference?





A t (or F) statistic grows with both the magnitude of the effect and the sample size — increase n with a fixed mean difference and t climbs accordingly. Effect sizes like Cohen’s d, r, or η² are standardised and largely insulated from sample size. That’s why a significant result with a tiny effect size can still be practically meaningless, and why both belong in your report.
→ Worked example with numbers





Post-hoc tests
A significant F-test only tells you that at least one pair of groups differs — not which pairs. Request post-hoc tests (e.g., Tukey HSD) or planned contrasts to identify specific differences. JASP provides both.


How to do it in JASP
Go to ANOVA → ANOVA in the analysis menu.






NoteJASP Video Library — One-Way ANOVA



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-oneway:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up one-way ANOVA, post-hoc tests
▶ Normality and Homogeneity Check — assumption checks
▶ Effect Size — requesting η² and ω²
▶ Descriptive Plots — visualising group means

title: ANOVA
text:
ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)

What is it?
ANCOVA extends ANOVA by adding one or more continuous covariates — variables you want to statistically control for. By removing the variance explained by the covariate, ANCOVA gives a more sensitive test of the group effect.
Example: comparing therapy outcomes across three groups while controlling for pre-therapy symptom severity.


Key additional assumption

Homogeneity of regression slopes: the relationship between the covariate and the DV should be the same in all groups. JASP can test this by adding a Group × Covariate interaction — it should not be significant.







TipHow ANCOVA partitions variance (with worked example)





Once you add a covariate, the regression coefficients for the group dummies no longer recover the raw group means — they recover means adjusted for the covariate. The F-tests for group and covariate then come from comparing the full model against reduced models that drop one predictor at a time.
→ Worked example: extraversion ~ nationality + openness





How to do it in JASP
Add covariates in the Covariates box when setting up an ANOVA.






NoteJASP Video Library — ANCOVA



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#ancova:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up ANCOVA, adding the covariate
▶ Add Interaction — testing homogeneity of regression slopes
▶ Descriptive Plots — marginal means after controlling for the covariate

title: ANOVA
text:
Factorial ANOVA

What is it?
Factorial ANOVA includes two or more between-subjects factors. In addition to the main effect of each factor, it tests the interaction — whether the effect of one factor depends on the level of another.
Example: testing whether a new intervention is effective (factor 1: intervention vs. control) and whether this depends on participant gender (factor 2: male/female/other).


Interpreting interactions
An interaction means the effect of factor A is different at different levels of factor B. Always examine interaction plots — JASP provides these under Descriptive plots. A significant interaction means you should interpret simple main effects rather than main effects alone.






TipWhat “interaction” really means





A concrete example: does the effect of alcohol on accidents differ across levels of speed? If somebody is going fast, maybe the alcohol effect is smaller (or larger) than at low speeds. That’s what “the effect of one variable depends on the level of another” looks like in practice.





How to do it in JASP






NoteJASP Video Library — Factorial ANOVA



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-factorial:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up a two-way ANOVA
▶ Plots — interaction plots
▶ Simple Main Effects — follow-up after a significant interaction
▶ Post-Hoc Tests — pairwise comparisons

title: ANOVA
text:
Repeated-measures ANOVA

What is it?
Repeated-measures ANOVA is used when the same participants are measured across multiple conditions or time points (within-subjects design). Because measurements within a person are correlated, this design is more powerful than a between-subjects design of the same size.
Mixed designs contain both a within-subjects factor (e.g., time: pre, post, follow-up) and a between-subjects factor (e.g., treatment group).


Key additional assumption: sphericity
For within-subjects factors with three or more levels, ANOVA assumes sphericity — that the variances of the differences between all pairs of levels are equal. JASP performs Mauchly’s test automatically:

If sphericity is violated (p < .05): use the Greenhouse-Geisser or Huynh-Feldt correction (JASP offers both)
If there are only two within-subjects levels, sphericity cannot be violated (it is automatic)







TipSphericity, Mauchly, and “just always apply the correction”





Sphericity = the variances of the differences between every pair of within-subject levels are equal. Mauchly’s test flags violations, but (like Levene’s and Shapiro-Wilk) it’s sensitive to sample size. The pragmatic move: under Assumption Checks → Sphericity corrections, tick Greenhouse-Geisser (or Huynh-Feldt) by default — they adjust the degrees of freedom in the ANOVA table to give you a result that’s robust to sphericity violations whether or not Mauchly is significant.





How to do it in JASP
Go to ANOVA → Repeated Measures ANOVA.






NoteJASP Video Library — Repeated-Measures ANOVA



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-repeated:

▶ Main Analysis — defining within-subjects factors and levels
▶ Sphericity Test — Mauchly’s test and Greenhouse-Geisser correction
▶ Plots — profile plots for within-subjects designs
▶ Contrasts — planned comparisons
▶ Post-Hoc Tests — pairwise comparisons with correction

title: ANOVA
text:
Interpreting the ANOVA output
The ANOVA table shows:







Column
What it means




F
Test statistic — variance between groups / variance within groups


df
Degrees of freedom — (number of groups − 1) for the effect, (N − number of groups) for the error


p
Significance — reject H₀ if p < α


η² or ω²
Effect size









TipAPA reporting



F(df_effect, df_error) = [value], p = [value], η² = [value].
Example: F(2, 57) = 4.32, p = .018, η² = .13.
For repeated measures with a correction: F(1.76, 46.5) = 5.21, p = .011, η² = .17 (Greenhouse-Geisser corrected).

title: ANOVA
text:
Non-parametric alternatives
If ANOVA assumptions are seriously violated:



ANOVA design
Non-parametric alternative




One-way (between)
Kruskal–Wallis test


One-way (within)
Friedman test



Both are available under ANOVA → Nonparametric in JASP.

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Basics
Regression allows you to model the relationship between a continuous outcome variable (the dependent variable, DV) and one or more predictors (the independent variables, IVs). Start with correlation to quantify the relationship between two variables, then move to regression when you want to predict an outcome or test the unique contribution of each predictor.
This page covers:

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Correlation

What is it?
A correlation coefficient (r) describes the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two continuous variables. Pearson’s r ranges from −1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive), with 0 indicating no linear relationship.



r
Conventional label




±.10
Small


±.30
Medium


±.50
Large





When to use it

As a descriptive measure of association between two variables (no implied causality)
As a preliminary step before regression
When reporting relationships in your results section



Assumptions


Both variables are continuous (interval/ratio)
The relationship is linear (check with a scatter plot — non-linear relationships yield misleading r values)
Approximately bivariate normal — no extreme outliers (outliers can inflate or deflate r dramatically)


If assumptions are violated, use Spearman’s ρ (rho) — a rank-based non-parametric alternative available in JASP.


How to do it in JASP
Go to Regression → Correlation.






NoteJASP Video Library — Correlation



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#correlation:

▶ Main Analysis — Pearson/Spearman correlation matrix
▶ Plots — scatter plots and correlation matrix visualisation
▶ Partial Correlation — controlling for a third variable

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Simple regression

What is it?
Simple (linear) regression models the relationship between one predictor (X) and a continuous outcome (Y):
\[Y = b_0 + b_1 X + \varepsilon\]

b₀: the intercept — the predicted value of Y when X = 0
b₁: the slope — the expected change in Y for a one-unit increase in X
ε: the residual error

The R² statistic tells you the proportion of variance in Y explained by the model. Adjusted R² penalises for the number of predictors and is used for model comparison.






TipWhy the intercept (b₀) belongs in the model



You might be tempted to drop the intercept when it isn’t substantively meaningful (e.g. when X = 0 makes no sense). Don’t — omitting it forces the regression line through the origin, which introduces systematic over- and under-estimation. The reported R² can then look misleadingly high because it’s compared against a degenerate null model that predicts zero rather than the mean.
→ Worked example with album sales




Assumptions


Linearity: the relationship between X and Y is linear — check with a scatter plot
Independence of residuals: each observation is independent — check with the Durbin-Watson statistic
Homoscedasticity: the spread of residuals is constant across fitted values — check the residuals vs. fitted plot
Normality of residuals: residuals should be approximately normally distributed — check the residuals Q-Q plot
No influential outliers: cases with high Cook’s distance (> 1 as a rough rule) warrant investigation








TipDiagnosing homoscedasticity at a glance





Plot the standardised residuals against the standardised predictions. A “healthy” cloud of points means error is constant across the range of Y. A funnel shape or a tilted line means the model is more accurate for some predicted values than others — a sign of mis-specification.
→ Visual catalogue: healthy vs. heteroscedastic plots





How to do it in JASP
Go to Regression → Linear Regression.






NoteJASP Video Library — Simple Regression



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#regression:

▶ Single Predictor — simple regression setup and output
▶ Assumption Checks — residual plots, Q-Q plots
▶ Cook’s Distance — identifying influential cases

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Multiple regression

What is it?
Multiple regression extends simple regression to two or more predictors:
\[Y = b_0 + b_1 X_1 + b_2 X_2 + \ldots + b_k X_k + \varepsilon\]
Each bⱼ is a partial regression coefficient — it represents the unique effect of predictor Xⱼ on Y, controlling for all other predictors in the model. This is a crucial distinction from simple regression or from looking at correlations in isolation.


Building your model
There are several approaches to entering predictors:







Method
When to use




Simultaneous (Enter)
All predictors entered at once. Use when you have theory-driven reasons to include all predictors


Hierarchical
Predictors entered in blocks based on theoretical priority. Change in R² (ΔR²) for each block tests the incremental contribution


Stepwise
Automated selection based on statistical criteria — generally discouraged for confirmatory research but can be useful exploratory









TipHierarchical regression in JASP





How to set up Model 0 → Model 1 → Model 2 in JASP’s Regression module, what the ΔR² between consecutive models actually means, and why “controlling for X” and “adding X to the null model” are the same operation.





Additional assumption: multicollinearity
When predictors are highly correlated with each other, regression coefficients become unstable. Check: - Tolerance (should be > .1) or VIF (variance inflation factor, should be < 10) — both available in JASP under Collinearity Diagnostics






TipVIF, tolerance, and why 10 isn’t a hard cutoff





What multicollinearity really means (predictors that overlap), how to read VIF (the 10 threshold is a continuum, not a binary pass/fail), and why a scatter plot matrix of your predictors is still worth its weight — VIF only catches linear dependence.





How to do it in JASP






NoteJASP Video Library — Multiple Regression



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#regression:

▶ Multiple Predictors — multiple regression setup and output
▶ Hierarchical Regression — entering predictors in blocks, ΔR²
▶ Bootstrap Confidence Intervals — robust inference for the coefficients

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Interpreting the regression output
The key tables in JASP output:
Model Summary







Column
What it means




R
Multiple correlation coefficient


R²
Proportion of variance in Y explained by all predictors combined


Adjusted R²
R² corrected for the number of predictors — use this for model comparison


RMSE
Root mean square error — average prediction error in the original units



Coefficients table







Column
What it means




b (Unstandardised)
The slope in original units — “one unit increase in X is associated with a b change in Y, holding all other predictors constant”


β (Standardised)
The slope in standard deviation units — comparable across predictors with different scales


t / p
Whether the slope is significantly different from zero


95% CI
Confidence interval for the slope









TipAPA reporting example



Album sales were significantly predicted by advertising budget, b = 0.096, 95% CI [0.076, 0.115], β = .578, t(196) = 9.98, p < .001. The model explained 33% of the variance in album sales, F(1, 198) = 99.6, p < .001, adjusted R² = .33.

title: Correlation & Regression
text:
Dummy coding categorical predictors
Continuous predictors enter regression directly. Categorical predictors must be dummy coded: create k − 1 binary variables (0/1), where k is the number of categories. JASP does this automatically when you define a variable as nominal.
Example: a variable with 3 groups (control, therapy A, therapy B) → 2 dummy variables. The reference category (control) becomes the baseline against which the others are compared.






TipHow predictions and group means come out of the dummies



The intercept becomes the mean of the reference group; each dummy’s coefficient is the difference from that group. Add a covariate (or another categorical predictor) and the coefficients shift slightly — the dummies no longer recover the group means exactly, but the prediction formula still works the same way. With an interaction term added, you do recover each cell mean — at the cost of more parameters.
→ Part 1: one categorical predictor + optional covariate · Part 2: two predictors and the interaction term

title: Contingency Tables
text:
Basics
When both your variables are categorical, the appropriate tools come from the chi-square family. A contingency table (cross-tabulation) shows the frequency of every combination of categories, and a chi-square test asks whether the two variables are independent.
This page covers:

title: Contingency Tables
text:
Chi-square test of independence

What is it?
The chi-square test of independence asks: are two categorical variables related, or independent? It compares the observed cell frequencies in a contingency table against the expected frequencies under the null hypothesis of independence.
\[\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}\]
Example: do voting preferences (Party A / Party B / Party C) differ across age groups (18–30, 31–50, 51+)? The contingency table is 3 × 3; the null hypothesis is that age group and party preference are independent.


When to use it

Both variables are categorical (nominal or ordinal treated as nominal)
Each participant contributes to exactly one cell of the table (one row per participant)
You want to test whether membership in one variable’s categories is related to membership in the other’s



Assumptions


Independence: each observation is independent of the others (no participant appears in more than one cell)
Expected cell counts: all expected counts should be ≥ 5 (a common rule of thumb); at minimum, no more than 20% of cells should have expected counts below 5
If expected counts are too small, use Fisher’s exact test instead (see below)




Effect size
Report Cramér’s V (or φ for a 2 × 2 table):



Cramér’s V
Conventional label (df = 1)




.10
Small


.30
Medium


.50
Large



For larger tables, the benchmarks scale down — consult DSUJ Chapter 19 or use JASP’s effect size output.


How to do it in JASP
Go to Frequencies → Contingency Tables in the analysis menu.






NoteJASP Video Library — Contingency Tables



Recordings at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Frequencies.html:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up the table, requesting chi-square
▶ Cells — observed counts, expected counts, residuals
▶ Statistics — Cramér’s V and other effect sizes





Interpreting the output







Statistic
What it means




χ²
The test statistic — bigger means observed frequencies deviate more from expected


df
(rows − 1) × (columns − 1)


p
Reject H₀ (independence) if p < α


Cramér’s V
Effect size — the strength of association



If the test is significant, inspect the standardised residuals to see which cells contribute most to the deviation from independence.






TipAPA reporting example



A chi-square test of independence showed a significant association between age group and party preference, χ²(4) = 18.6, p < .001, Cramér’s V = .24. Inspection of the standardised residuals indicated that voters aged 51+ were over-represented among Party A supporters.

title: Contingency Tables
text:
Chi-square goodness-of-fit
A variant of the chi-square test for one categorical variable against a hypothesised distribution. Example: does a die roll uniformly (each face expected at 1/6) over 600 throws?
In JASP: Frequencies → Multinomial Test. Specify either equal expected probabilities or a custom expected distribution.

title: Contingency Tables
text:
Fisher’s exact test
When expected cell counts are too small for the chi-square approximation to be trustworthy (typically a 2 × 2 table with any expected count < 5), Fisher’s exact test computes the exact probability of the observed table under the null. It is available in the same JASP dialog — just tick Fisher’s exact test under Statistics.






TipWhy Fisher’s exact, not chi-square, with small expected counts





The chi-square statistic only approximately follows the χ² distribution; the approximation breaks down when expected counts are small. Fisher’s exact test side-steps the approximation altogether by enumerating every possible table with the same row and column totals. It costs nothing in interpretation — you still report a p-value — and is the safer choice for sparse tables.

title: Contingency Tables
text:
Bayesian alternative
JASP also offers a Bayesian contingency table analysis, which returns a Bayes factor for the alternative model (variables are related) over the null (independent). Useful when you want to quantify evidence for either hypothesis, including evidence for independence.






NoteJASP Video Library — Bayesian Contingency Tables




▶ Bayesian Contingency Tables — setting up the analysis, interpreting the Bayes factor

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
Basics
Before running any inferential test, always explore your data. Descriptive statistics and visualisations help you understand the shape of your distributions, identify outliers, and check whether your data meet the assumptions of the analyses you plan to run.
This is not a step to skip — surprising patterns often only become visible at this stage.

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
What are descriptive statistics?
Descriptive statistics summarise the key features of a dataset without making inferences about a population. The most important are:
Central tendency — where is the middle of the distribution?



Statistic
Use when




Mean
Continuous data, approximately symmetric distribution


Median
Skewed distributions or ordinal data


Mode
Categorical data, or to describe the most common value



Spread — how much do values vary?







Statistic
What it tells you




Standard deviation (SD)
Average distance from the mean (same units as the data)


Variance
SD² — less intuitive but used in many formulas


Range
Max − Min — sensitive to outliers


Interquartile range (IQR)
Middle 50% of values — robust to outliers



Shape — is the distribution symmetric?







Statistic
What it tells you




Skewness
Whether the tail is longer on the left (negative) or right (positive)


Kurtosis
How heavy or light the tails are relative to a normal distribution

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
When to use descriptive statistics
Always — as the first step in every analysis. Specifically:

Before any test: report means and SDs for your key variables
To check normality: look at histograms and Q-Q plots, and inspect skewness/kurtosis values
To detect outliers: boxplots flag values beyond 1.5 × IQR from the quartiles
To describe your sample: demographics (age, gender, group sizes) always require descriptives

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
Assumptions to check
Descriptive statistics have no assumptions themselves, but examining them tells you whether the assumptions of your next analysis hold:


Normality: histograms should be roughly bell-shaped; skewness and kurtosis should be close to 0 (rough rule: |value| < 1 for reasonable samples, though this depends on sample size)
Outliers: inspect boxplots — individual points beyond the whiskers warrant investigation
Floor/ceiling effects: if most values cluster at the minimum or maximum, your scale may not have sufficient range

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP
Open your dataset, go to Descriptives → Descriptives in the analysis menu.






NoteJASP Video Library — Descriptives



All screen recordings are at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Descriptives.html:

▶ Main Analysis — setting up descriptives, requesting mean, SD, and frequency tables
▶ Split Variable — computing descriptives separately per group
▶ Raincloud Plots — raw data + density + boxplot in one figure
▶ Distribution and Boxplots — exploring spread and outliers
▶ Scatter Plot — visualising relationships between two continuous variables
▶ Assumption Checks — Q-Q plots and normality tests

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:
Interpreting the output
After running Descriptives in JASP, the output table shows your requested statistics. Key things to check:

N: the number of valid (non-missing) observations per variable
Mean ± SD: the central tendency and spread — always report these together
Skewness / Kurtosis: both should be close to 0 for a normal distribution. Divide each value by its standard error to get a z-score; |z| > 3.29 flags a significant deviation (in large samples, use visual inspection instead)
Boxplot whiskers: extend to 1.5 × IQR; individual points beyond the whiskers are potential outliers







TipReporting in APA format



For continuous variables: M = [mean], SD = [SD], N = [n]. For frequency data: report counts and percentages.

title: Descriptive Statistics & Visualisation
text:

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Basics
Non-parametric tests are rank-based alternatives to t-tests and ANOVA. They don’t assume the data follow a particular distribution — making them the go-to choice when the normality assumption is seriously violated (often in small samples, or with strong outliers or floor/ceiling effects).
This page covers the four most commonly used non-parametric tests for group comparison, each one a counterpart to a parametric procedure you already know:

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
What do they actually do?
All four tests follow the same idea: convert your raw scores to ranks, then test whether the ranks differ between groups (or conditions). Because ranks are bounded and don’t depend on the original metric, the resulting test statistic isn’t pulled around by outliers or skew the way a t or F statistic can be.






TipWhy “rank” the data?





Imagine one participant in your dataset reports a reaction time of 12,000 ms while everyone else is between 200–800 ms. That single value drags the mean of their group up and inflates the variance of the difference — your t drops, your p climbs, and you may miss a real effect. Replace each raw score by its rank (1 = fastest, N = slowest), and that outlier becomes “the slowest one” — its influence is bounded. You trade away some information (the absolute spacing of the values) for robustness against extreme scores.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
section: When to use a non-parametric test
text:
When to use a non-parametric test

The normality assumption of the parametric test is seriously violated, especially in small samples (n < ~30) where you can’t lean on the central limit theorem
You have ordinal data (e.g. Likert items treated strictly as ranks), where means aren’t really meaningful
You have strong outliers that you don’t want to remove and that distort the parametric test
Sample sizes are small enough that visual checks (Q-Q plots, histograms per group) clearly look non-normal







WarningNon-parametric ≠ assumption-free



The Mann–Whitney test does not test for equal medians unless the two distributions have the same shape. If group A is symmetric and group B is heavily skewed, a significant Mann–Whitney result could reflect that difference in shape — not a median shift. Always inspect the distributions before interpreting.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
section: How to do them in JASP
text:
How to do them in JASP
In JASP, every non-parametric test lives alongside its parametric counterpart:

Wilcoxon rank-sum / signed-rank → T-Tests menu → tick the Wilcoxon option
Kruskal–Wallis → ANOVA menu → tick the Kruskal-Wallis test option (under Nonparametrics)
Friedman → Repeated Measures ANOVA menu → tick the Friedman option

JASP reports the rank-based statistic, a p-value, and a rank-biserial correlation as the effect size for the Wilcoxon tests.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Interpreting the output








Test
Statistic
Effect size




Wilcoxon rank-sum
W (or U)
rank-biserial correlation


Wilcoxon signed-rank
V
rank-biserial correlation (matched-rank)


Kruskal–Wallis
H (≈ χ²)
ε² or η²H


Friedman
χ²F
Kendall’s W



Report the test statistic, p-value, and an effect size. Where relevant, also report group medians (and IQRs) rather than means.






TipAPA reporting example



A Wilcoxon rank-sum test showed that reaction times were significantly higher in the alcohol condition (Mdn = 540, IQR = 180) than in the control condition (Mdn = 420, IQR = 140), W = 312, p = .008, rank-biserial r = .42.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Post-hoc tests
A significant Kruskal–Wallis or Friedman test tells you that at least two groups (conditions) differ — but not which. JASP offers Dunn’s post-hoc tests with Bonferroni or Holm correction directly in the same dialog.

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:

title: Non-Parametric Tests
text:
Try it yourself
Each exercise below ends with a collapsible solution. Try the analysis first, then expand to compare.

Exercise 1 (Wilcoxon signed-rank) — Do male quails fertilise more eggs after signaled vs control mating contexts?
Each male quail experienced two mating contexts (signaled vs control) in counterbalanced order, and the number of fertilised eggs was counted. Using leni_15a.jasp, run a Wilcoxon signed-rank test on the within-subject difference, and report the rank-biserial effect size alongside the medians.






Note▸ Worked solution






Walkthrough: Labcoat Leni — Having a quail of a time?
Annotated JASP output: leni_15a.html






Exercise 2 (Kruskal–Wallis + Dunn post-hoc) — Do three types of “quail fetish” differ in fertilisation rate?
Male quails were classified into three fetish groups based on their behaviour around a terrycloth object, and fertilisation rate was compared across the three groups. Using leni_15b.jasp, fit a Kruskal–Wallis test and follow up with Dunn’s post-hoc (Bonferroni-corrected).






Note▸ Worked solution






Walkthrough: Labcoat Leni — Eggs-traordinary
Annotated JASP output: leni_15b.html






Exercise 3 (Mann–Whitney / Wilcoxon rank-sum) — Do men and women spend different amounts of time shopping?
The shopping.jasp dataset has shopping-trip durations (in minutes) for male and female participants. Run a Mann–Whitney test (i.e. Wilcoxon rank-sum) and interpret the result alongside the group medians.






Note▸ Worked solution






Walkthrough: Smart Alex Task 15.7
Annotated JASP output: alex_15_07-08.html

title: t-Tests
text:
Basics
t-tests are used to compare means — either against a known value, or between two groups. They are one of the most widely used inferential tests in psychology.

title: t-Tests
text:
Three types of t-test








Test
Research question
Example




One-sample
Does my sample mean differ from a known value?
Is the mean IQ of my sample different from the population mean of 100?


Paired-samples
Do two related measurements differ?
Do anxiety scores decrease from pre- to post-therapy within the same participants?


Independent-samples
Do two independent groups differ?
Do males and females differ in their extraversion scores?

title: t-Tests
text:
When to use which t-test

One-sample: you have a single group and want to compare its mean to a fixed reference value (e.g., population norm, chance level, zero)
Paired-samples (also called dependent-samples or repeated-measures t-test): the same participants measured twice, or participants matched in pairs — the two measurements are not independent
Independent-samples: two separate, unrelated groups — each participant contributes one score







WarningDon’t confuse paired and independent



Using an independent-samples t-test when you have a repeated-measures design throws away half the information and inflates your error term. Always identify whether measurements within rows of your dataset are linked.








TipThe paired t-test is just a one-sample t-test on difference scores





For each participant, compute the difference between their two measurements. Now you have one set of difference scores — and you’re back to a one-sample t-test against H₀: mean difference = 0. This is why “paired”, “dependent-samples”, and “within-subjects” all refer to the same procedure.

title: t-Tests
text:
One-sided or two-sided?
Whichever t-test you pick, you’ll also need to decide what alternative hypothesis to test:

Two-sided (default in JASP): H₁ says the mean differs from the null value in either direction. Use this unless you have a strong directional prediction.
One-sided (directional): H₁ says the mean is specifically greater than, or specifically less than, the null value. More statistical power if you’re right about the direction — but if the effect goes the other way, you can’t reject H₀.







TipA one-sided test is a commitment, not a shortcut



Commit to a one-sided test before looking at your data — ideally pre-registered. Switching to one-sided after seeing a result in your favoured direction is a common form of p-hacking.
→ Visual explainer: how the rejection region changes between one-sided and two-sided








TipChoosing your alternative hypothesis





Using the IQ self-assessment example: two-sided (“self-reported IQ is different from 120”) asks whether there’s any bias, while one-sided (“students systematically overestimate IQ”) tests a specific direction. Different research questions, different rejection regions.

title: t-Tests
text:
Assumptions


Scale data: the dependent variable is continuous (interval or ratio level)
Independence of observations: each row in the dataset is an independent participant (violated in paired designs, which is why you use the paired t-test instead)
Normality: the dependent variable (or, for paired data, the difference scores) should be approximately normally distributed, especially important in small samples (n < 30)
Equal variances (independent-samples only): Levene’s test checks this. JASP reports a Welch correction by default, which is robust to unequal variances — this is generally the safer choice








TipWhy “always use Welch” is the safer default





A subtle point: significance tests for assumptions (Levene’s, Shapiro-Wilk) are themselves driven by sample size. At large n, even a trivial deviation from equal variances will make Levene’s significant — that doesn’t mean you have a “problem”. At small n, neither test has the power to detect a meaningful violation. The recommended workflow: look at the observed standard deviations (a ratio of more than ~1.4 between groups is worth noting), inspect Q-Q plots, and just default to the Welch t-test — it costs you a little power and buys robustness against unequal variances.




Checking normality
Use the Q-Q plot and inspect skewness/kurtosis in Descriptives (see the Descriptives page). The Shapiro-Wilk test is available in JASP under the assumption checks, but is sensitive to sample size — a visual check is often more informative.

title: t-Tests
text:
Effect size
Always report an effect size alongside your p-value. For t-tests, use Cohen’s d:



d
Conventional label




0.2
Small


0.5
Medium


0.8
Large



These benchmarks are rough guides — the meaningful effect size depends on your research context. Tick Cohen’s d under Effect Size in the JASP options panel.

title: t-Tests
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP
Go to T-Tests in the analysis menu and select the appropriate test.






NoteJASP Video Library — T-Tests



All screen recordings are at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_T-Tests.html:
Independent-samples t-test

▶ Main Analysis — setting up the test, grouping variable
▶ Assumption Checks — normality and homogeneity of variance
▶ Effect Size — requesting Cohen’s d and confidence intervals
▶ Plots — raincloud or bar plots for group differences

One-sample and paired-samples t-test

▶ One-Sample — comparing a mean to a fixed reference value
▶ Paired-Samples — pre/post measurements or matched pairs

title: t-Tests
text:
Interpreting the output
The JASP output for an independent-samples t-test contains:

t: the test statistic — the ratio of the mean difference to the standard error of the difference
df: degrees of freedom (for Welch’s test, this will not be a whole number)
p: the two-tailed p-value. Reject H₀ (no difference) if p < your α level (typically .05)
Mean difference: the raw difference between group means — report this alongside the SE or CI
Cohen’s d: the effect size







TipAPA reporting example



An independent-samples t-test showed that the anxiety ratings of participants shown a picture of a spider (M = 47.0, SD = 9.3) were significantly higher than those of participants shown a picture of a real spider (M = 40.0, SD = 9.3), t(21.4) = 1.68, p = .108, d = 0.71. (This is an example — adjust numbers and interpretation to your own results.)

title: t-Tests
text:
Non-parametric alternative
If your data strongly violate the normality assumption (and your sample is small), use the Wilcoxon tests instead:



t-test
Non-parametric equivalent




One-sample
Wilcoxon signed-rank test


Paired-samples
Wilcoxon signed-rank test


Independent-samples
Mann–Whitney U test (Wilcoxon rank-sum test)



These are available under T-Tests → Nonparametric in JASP.

title: t-Tests
text:
Further reading

JASP blog — Classical one-sample t-test
JASP blog — Classical independent-samples t-test

text:

text:

Exercise 1 — Is arachnophobia specific to real spiders, or do pictures evoke similar anxiety?
Twelve arachnophobes were each exposed to a real tarantula and a picture of one, with their anxiety rated in both conditions. Using big_hairy_spider.jasp, run both an independent-samples and a paired-samples t-test on the same data. Compare the conclusions — what does the discrepancy reveal about respecting the design?

title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
A statistics refresher for master’s psychology students at UvA
Before you dive into your thesis or specialist master’s courses, make sure you are comfortable with the analyses you will actually use. This site is your self-study companion — work through topics at your own pace, watch short JASP demonstrations, and practise with real datasets.

title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
How to use this site

Start with Getting Started if you have not used JASP before, or need a quick refresh on loading data and navigating the interface.
Work through Basics — descriptive statistics, t-tests, ANOVA, regression, non-parametric tests, and contingency tables. These are the foundations that almost every analysis builds on.
Consult Advanced Topics when your thesis or course requires a specific technique. Not everyone needs all of these — pick what is relevant to you.

Each topic page follows the same structure:






NoteWhat you will find on each topic page




What is it? — a short conceptual overview
When to use it — decision guide: which research question, which data type
Assumptions — what to check before interpreting your results
How to do it in JASP — screen recordings
Interpreting the output — what to report and how to read the numbers
Further reading — DSUJ chapter, blog posts
Try it yourself — downloadable JASP file and practice exercises

title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
Basics
These topics cover the analyses expected of every master’s student.


Descriptive Statistics
Summarise and visualise your data before running any inferential test. Mean, SD, histograms, boxplots, raincloud plots.


t-Tests
Compare one or two means. One-sample, paired-samples, and independent-samples t-tests — with effect sizes and assumption checks.


ANOVA
Compare three or more groups, or multiple factors. One-way, factorial, ANCOVA, and repeated-measures ANOVA.


Correlation & Regression
Quantify relationships and predict outcomes. Pearson correlation, simple regression, and multiple regression.


Non-Parametric Tests
Rank-based alternatives when normality is violated. Mann–Whitney, Wilcoxon signed-rank, Kruskal–Wallis, and Friedman.


Contingency Tables
Test associations between two categorical variables. Chi-square tests of independence, Fisher’s exact, and Cramér’s V.

title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
Advanced Topics
These analyses appear frequently in master’s theses. Choose the ones relevant to your research area.


Mediation & Moderation
Test whether an effect works through a third variable (mediation) or depends on a third variable (moderation). Uses the PROCESS module.


Logistic Regression
Predict a binary outcome (yes/no, clinical/control) from one or more predictors. Odds ratios and classification accuracy.


Factor Analysis
Identify underlying dimensions in a set of items. Exploratory (EFA) and Confirmatory (CFA) approaches, plus PCA.


Power Analysis
Determine how many participants you need to detect an effect of a given size. Avoid underpowered studies.


Missing Data & Imputation
Understand the types of missingness (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) and when multiple imputation is appropriate.


Meta-Analysis
Synthesise effect sizes across studies. Forest plots, heterogeneity, and moderator analysis in JASP.








Note



Coming soon: Measurement Invariance, Path Modeling, Latent Class / Profile Analysis, and Network Analysis. These are more specialised topics currently being developed.

title: Welcome to the Paddling Pool
text:
Resources



Resource
Description




Discovering Statistics Using JASP (DSUJ)
The main textbook this site is built around. Available in the UvA library.


How to Use JASP
Step-by-step blog posts for specific analyses on the JASP website.


JASP Download
Free, open-source statistical software used throughout this site.


JASP Video Library
The full collection of screen recordings we draw from.

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Advanced Topics
Factor analysis is a family of techniques for identifying latent dimensions (factors) that underlie a set of observed variables. It is widely used in scale development, construct validation, and data reduction.
Three related techniques are covered here:

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA)

When to use it

You have a set of items (e.g., questionnaire items) and you do not know — or want to discover — how many factors underlie them
Scale development: determining the dimensional structure of a new measure
Generating hypotheses about construct structure to be tested later with CFA



Key decisions
How many factors?

Scree plot: look for the “elbow” — the point where the eigenvalues flatten out
Kaiser criterion: retain factors with eigenvalue > 1 (can over-extract in large datasets)
Parallel analysis: compare actual eigenvalues to those from random data — more accurate than the Kaiser criterion (recommended)

Rotation method
Rotation makes factors more interpretable. Two families:








Family
Method
Use when




Orthogonal
Varimax
You expect factors to be uncorrelated


Oblique
Oblimin / Promax
You expect factors to correlate (common in psychology)



In psychology, oblique rotation is usually more realistic.
What loadings tell you
A factor loading is the correlation between an item and a factor. Loadings > .30 (or .40 for a more conservative threshold) are typically considered meaningful. An item should load strongly on one factor and minimally on others — check for cross-loadings.


How to do it in JASP
Go to Factor → Exploratory Factor Analysis.






NoteJASP Video Library — EFA



Recording at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Factor-Analysis.html#efa:

▶ EFA Main Analysis — number of factors, rotation method, factor loadings, scree plot

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA)

When to use it

You have a theoretically specified model of which items load on which factors
Testing whether a previously validated scale replicates in your sample
Comparing alternative factor structures (e.g., one-factor vs. two-factor)
As a prerequisite to testing measurement invariance across groups



Model fit indices








Index
Acceptable range
Notes




χ²
Non-significant (p > .05)
Very sensitive to sample size — rarely met with large N


CFI / TLI
> .90 (acceptable), > .95 (good)
Comparative Fit Index / Tucker-Lewis Index


RMSEA
< .08 (acceptable), < .05 (good)
Root Mean Square Error of Approximation


SRMR
< .08
Standardised Root Mean Square Residual









TipAlways report multiple fit indices



No single fit index is sufficient. Report CFI, TLI, RMSEA (with 90% CI), and SRMR together.




How to do it in JASP
Go to Factor → Confirmatory Factor Analysis.






NoteJASP Video Library — CFA



Recording at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Factor-Analysis.html#cfa:

▶ CFA Main Analysis — specifying a CFA model, fit indices, modification indices

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

When to use it

You want to reduce a large number of variables to a smaller set of components for use in further analyses
You want to remove multicollinearity by replacing correlated variables with orthogonal components
You are not primarily interested in a latent variable model



How to do it in JASP
Go to Factor → Principal Component Analysis.






NoteJASP Video Library — PCA



Recording at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Factor-Analysis.html#pca:

▶ PCA Main Analysis — number of components, rotation, component loadings and scores

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Reliability: Cronbach’s α
When developing or using a scale, report Cronbach’s α as a measure of internal consistency. Values > .70 are conventionally considered acceptable.
Cronbach’s α is available in JASP under Factor → Reliability Analysis. Note that α depends on the number of items and their average inter-correlation, so it is not a pure measure of unidimensionality.

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Further reading

DSUJ Chapter on factor analysis (check your edition) — EFA and CFA with JASP
Brown, T. A. (2015). Confirmatory factor analysis for applied research. Guilford Press.

title: Factor Analysis
text:
Try it yourself

Dataset: exam_anxiety.jasp — exam performance, anxiety, and revision time. Explore correlations between scale variables as a starting point before fitting a factor model.







NoteDSUJ exercises



The factor analysis chapter self-tests and Smart Alex tasks are available with solutions at the discoverjasp companion site.

title: Network Analysis
text: Advanced Topics

title: Network Analysis
text:
🚧
This page is under construction.
Content covering psychometric network analysis will be added here.

title: Network Analysis
text:
What this page will cover
Psychometric network analysis conceptualises psychological constructs as networks of interacting components rather than as reflections of a single latent variable. For example, depression might be modelled as a network of interrelated symptoms (insomnia, fatigue, anhedonia, etc.) rather than as a single underlying factor.
Key methods:

GGM (Gaussian Graphical Model): estimates partial correlations between variables, with edges representing direct relationships after controlling for all other variables
EBIC graphical LASSO: a regularisation method to estimate sparse networks and avoid over-fitting
Centrality measures: which nodes (variables) are most central to the network? (strength, betweenness, closeness)
Community detection: identifying clusters of closely connected nodes

JASP has a Network Analysis module.

title: Network Analysis
text:
Useful resources in the meantime

Borsboom, D., & Cramer, A. O. J. (2013). Network analysis: An integrative approach to the structure of psychopathology. Annual Review of Clinical Psychology, 9, 91–121.
Epskamp, S., & Fried, E. I. (2018). A tutorial on regularized partial correlation networks. Psychological Methods, 23(4), 617–634.
The qgraph R package: cran.r-project.org/package=qgraph

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Advanced Topics
Missing data is almost inevitable in psychology research. How you handle it matters: the wrong approach can introduce bias and reduce statistical power. This page covers the types of missingness and the options available for dealing with it.

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Types of missingness
Before choosing a method, you need to think about why data are missing.








Mechanism
Definition
Example




MCAR — Missing Completely At Random
The probability of being missing is unrelated to any variable (observed or unobserved)
A random equipment failure causes some data to be lost


MAR — Missing At Random
The probability of being missing depends on observed variables, but not on the missing value itself
Older participants more likely to skip a question about technology use, but we observe age


MNAR — Missing Not At Random
The probability of being missing depends on the missing value itself (or unobserved variables)
Participants with the highest depression scores are most likely to drop out of a depression study









WarningWhy this matters




MCAR: listwise deletion is unbiased (but wasteful)
MAR: listwise deletion is biased. Multiple imputation or FIML is appropriate
MNAR: all standard methods can produce biased results. Requires sensitivity analysis or specialised models.



Testing MCAR: Little’s MCAR test (available in SPSS and R; not yet in JASP). In practice, MCAR is often implausible — assume MAR unless you have strong reasons otherwise.

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Common approaches

1. Listwise deletion (complete-case analysis)
Only cases with complete data on all variables are included. This is the JASP default.

When it is acceptable: MCAR and sample size remains adequate
Problem: throws away all information from partially complete cases; biased under MAR



2. Pairwise deletion
Each analysis uses all cases available for the variables involved. Different analyses may use different subsets.

Problem: can lead to inconsistent estimates and negative definite correlation matrices



3. Mean substitution
Replace missing values with the variable mean.

Avoid this: it distorts the distribution, reduces variance, and biases correlations. It is almost never appropriate.



4. Multiple Imputation (MI) ✓ Recommended
MI creates m complete datasets by replacing each missing value with m plausible values drawn from the predictive distribution of the missing data given the observed data. Each dataset is analysed separately, and results are pooled using Rubin’s rules.
Steps: 1. Impute: create m datasets (typically 20–100, though m = 5 is still common in older literature; more is better) 2. Analyse: run your intended analysis on each imputed dataset 3. Pool: combine estimates across datasets — JASP does this automatically






TipKey principle



The imputation model should include all variables you will use in the analysis, plus any auxiliary variables that predict missingness. A sparse imputation model produces biased results.


In JASP: go to Missing Values → Multiple Imputation. You can then run subsequent analyses that automatically use the imputed datasets.


5. Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML)
FIML estimates model parameters using all available information — including partially complete cases — without creating imputed datasets. It is the preferred approach for structural equation models (SEM) and is available in JASP’s SEM module.



Approach
Valid under
JASP availability




Listwise deletion
MCAR only
Default


Multiple Imputation
MAR (and MCAR)
Missing Values module


FIML
MAR (and MCAR)
SEM module

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Diagnosing missingness in JASP

Descriptives: the N in the descriptives table shows how many valid values each variable has. Compare to total N.
Missing value plots: available in the Missing Values module — visualise which variables and cases have missing data, and whether missingness is patterned.

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Reporting






TipReporting missing data



State (a) the percentage of missing data per variable, (b) the missingness mechanism assumed (MCAR/MAR/MNAR) and why, and (c) the method used to handle it. Example: “Data were missing on [variable] for 12% of participants. Little’s MCAR test was non-significant (χ²(5) = 4.2, p = .52), and missingness appeared unrelated to observed study variables, so listwise deletion was used.”

title: Missing Data & Imputation
text:
Further reading

Van Buuren, S. (2018). Flexible Imputation of Missing Data (2nd ed.). CRC Press. — Freely available at stefvanbuuren.name/fimd/
Sterne, J. A. C., et al. (2009). Multiple imputation for missing data in epidemiological and clinical research. BMJ, 338, b2393.
DSUJ Chapter 6 — Bias — includes discussion of data problems including missingness

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Advanced Topics
Use logistic regression when your outcome variable is categorical — most commonly binary (yes/no, clinical/control, recovered/not recovered). Linear regression is inappropriate for binary outcomes because it can produce predicted probabilities below 0 or above 1, and it violates homoscedasticity.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
What is it?
Binary logistic regression models the log-odds (logit) of the outcome as a linear function of the predictors:
\[\log\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = b_0 + b_1 X_1 + b_2 X_2 + \ldots\]
The model predicts the probability that a case belongs to the “outcome = 1” category. Predictors can be continuous, binary, or categorical (with dummy coding).

Odds ratios
The exponentiated coefficients (e^b) are odds ratios (OR):







OR
Interpretation




> 1
A one-unit increase in X is associated with increased odds of the outcome


< 1
A one-unit increase in X is associated with decreased odds of the outcome


= 1
No association









NoteOdds ≠ probability



An OR of 2 does not mean the outcome is “twice as likely” — it means the odds are doubled. Probabilities and odds are related but not the same. Always report predicted probabilities or absolute risk when communicating results to a non-statistical audience.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
When to use logistic regression







Situation
Appropriate method




Binary outcome (2 categories)
Binary logistic regression


Nominal outcome (3+ unordered categories)
Multinomial logistic regression


Ordinal outcome (3+ ordered categories)
Ordinal logistic regression



This page focuses on binary logistic regression — the most common case in psychology research.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Assumptions


Binary outcome: the DV must have exactly two categories
Independence of observations: each participant contributes one row
No multicollinearity: predictors should not be highly correlated — check VIF as in regular regression
Sufficient cell sizes: especially important when predictors are categorical; very small cells can cause convergence problems
No complete separation: if a predictor perfectly predicts the outcome, the model cannot estimate coefficients
Linearity of continuous predictors with the log-odds: check using Box-Tidwell test or visual inspection (available as an option in JASP)


Note: logistic regression does not require normality of residuals or homoscedasticity — these are linear regression assumptions that do not apply here.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Model fit
Unlike linear regression, logistic regression uses maximum likelihood estimation. Model fit statistics:







Statistic
What it tells you




−2 Log Likelihood (−2LL)
Overall fit — smaller is better


Nagelkerke R²
Pseudo-R²: approximate proportion of variance explained — treat as an effect size, not a literal R²


Hosmer-Lemeshow test
Goodness of fit — a non-significant result indicates adequate fit


Classification table
Percentage of cases correctly classified by the model



Model comparison: test whether adding predictors improves fit using the likelihood ratio test (χ² difference in −2LL with corresponding df).

title: Logistic Regression
section: How to do it in JASP
text:
How to do it in JASP
Go to Regression → Logistic Regression.






NoteJASP Video Library — Logistic Regression



Recording at jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#logistic-regression:

▶ Logistic Regression — setup, odds ratios, classification table, model fit

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Interpreting the output
Omnibus test: tests whether the model with predictors fits better than an intercept-only model. A significant χ² (p < .05) indicates the model explains a significant amount of variance.
Coefficients table: for each predictor, report: - b: the log-odds coefficient (hard to interpret directly) - OR (= e^b): the odds ratio — more interpretable - 95% CI for the OR: does it include 1.0? If not, the predictor is significant - p-value






TipAPA reporting example



Logistic regression was used to predict survival from age and treatment group. The model was significant, χ²(2) = 14.3, p < .001, Nagelkerke R² = .23. Treatment group was a significant predictor, b = 1.42, OR = 4.14, 95% CI [1.87, 9.17], p = .001.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Multinomial logistic regression
When the outcome has three or more unordered categories, use multinomial logistic regression. This runs separate binary logistic regressions comparing each category to a reference category. Available in JASP under Regression → Logistic Regression by changing the outcome type.

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Further reading

DSUJ Chapter 17 — Logistic regression — binary and multinomial logistic regression with JASP walkthroughs

title: Logistic Regression
text:
Try it yourself
Each exercise below ends with a collapsible solution. Try the analysis first, then expand to compare.

Exercise 1 (binary logistic regression — risk factors) — Which factors predict adolescent suicide risk?
Adolescents at risk of self-harm were measured on demographics, family environment, and music preferences (heavy metal among others). Using leni_17.jasp, fit a binary logistic regression with risk status as the outcome. Report each predictor’s odds ratio with its 95% CI and the Nagelkerke R².

Exercise 2 (binary logistic regression — developmental psychology) — Do age and theory-of-mind predict mastery of emotional display rules?
A psychologist measured whether each child could correctly apply a “display rule” (pass/fail), their age in months, and their theory-of-mind score. Using display.jasp, fit a logistic regression with display-rule mastery as the binary outcome and age + theory-of-mind as predictors.

Exercise 3 (binary logistic regression — social psychology) — Does social class (inferred from car) predict whether a driver cuts in?
Piff et al. (2012) ranked drivers’ social class by the type of car they drove (1–5) and observed whether each driver cut in front of another at a busy intersection. Using piff_2012_vehicle.jasp, fit a logistic regression with vehicle_cut (yes/no) as the outcome and vehicle (1–5) as the predictor. Interpret the odds ratio for a one-step increase in vehicle class.






Note▸ Worked solution

text:
🚧

title: Measurement Invariance
text:
What this page will cover
Measurement invariance (also called measurement equivalence) tests whether a scale measures the same construct in the same way across different groups (e.g., cultures, genders, time points). It is a prerequisite for meaningfully comparing latent means or factor structures across groups.
Levels of invariance:








Level
What is constrained
What it allows




Configural
Nothing (same factor structure)
Comparing factor structure qualitatively


Metric
Factor loadings equal across groups
Comparing relationships (correlations, regressions)


Scalar
Loadings + intercepts equal
Comparing latent means


Strict
Loadings + intercepts + residuals equal
Rarely required in practice



In practice: if scalar invariance holds, you can compare questionnaire means across groups. If only metric invariance holds (some intercepts differ = partial invariance), you can compare relationships but not means.

title: Measurement Invariance
